titleStmt.title: First Example: Reger Page 6 Hello World from Harmony!

sourceDesc.p: Max Reger: Supplement to the Theory of Modulation (translated by J. Bernhoff). Leipzig: C. F. Kahnt Nachfolger, 1904.

body.head: Analysis of the Examples in Modulation.

div.head: 1) G-major.
div.p:
Tonic C-major; use this C-major which is
at the same time the sub-dominant of G-major.
(Cadence!) oh yeah!
$$[C^{I}\;(=G^{IV}),\;G^{\underline{II}*},\;G^{V^{6}_{4}},\;G^{V},\;|G^{I}]$$

div.head: 2) D-major.
div.p:
Tonic C-major; relative (e-minor) to the domi-
nant (G-major) of C-major; use this e-minor
(1st inversion), which is also relative to the
subdominant (G-major) of D-major. (Cadence!)
$$[C^{I},\;C^{\underline{III}}\;(=D^{\underline{II}}),\;D^{V^{6}_{4}},\;D^{V},\;|D^{I}]$$

*) "-" this mark under the Roman cipher means: 1st in-
version (chord of the sixth),
"=" this mark under the Roman cipher means
2nd inversion (chord of the six-four).
